items.q: If an object is moving at constant velocity in a straight line, what is the net force on it?
items.correct: Zero — by Newton's first law, constant velocity requires zero net force.
items.misconception: There must be a forward force on the object to keep it moving.
items.random_wrong: The net force equals the velocity times the mass of the object.
items.why: This is the classic 'impetus' misconception — everyday experience (objects slow down due to friction) leads students to believe motion requires a continuous force.

items.q: When you push a wall, what does Newton's third law say about the wall?
items.correct: The wall exerts an equal and opposite force on you at the same instant.
items.misconception: The wall only pushes back if your force is strong enough to make it react.
items.random_wrong: The wall pushes back with a force proportional to how stiff it is, not equal to your force.
items.why: Students think action-reaction is causal and sequential — push first, then the wall 'responds' — instead of being two aspects of one interaction.

items.q: Does Hooke's law apply to a spring for any amount of stretching?
items.correct: No — it only holds up to the limit of proportionality; beyond that the relationship between force and extension is no longer linear.
items.misconception: Yes — Hooke's law applies to any spring at any extension.
items.random_wrong: Only when the force is small enough to be ignored.
items.why: Students apply F = kx universally without noting the limit of proportionality, leading to over-prediction at large extensions.

items.q: What is the SI unit of momentum?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: kg·m/s. “m/s²” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A crumple zone reduces injury by:
items.why: Correct answer: Increasing collision time. “Increasing force” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A 0.5 kg ball travels at 8 m/s. What is its momentum, in kg·m/s?
items.why: Exact answer: 4. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
